original_abstract: Traditional transition-based dependency parsers rely heavily on manual feature engineering, typically resulting in millions of sparse indicator features. These systems suffer from high computational overhead during feature extraction and poor generalization to unseen data. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural network-based classifier designed for a greedy, transition-based dependency parser. Our approach represents words, part-of-speech tags, and dependency labels as low-dimensional dense vectors. By utilizing a compact set of these dense features and a novel cube activation function to capture high-order interactions, our model achieves significant improvements in both accuracy and efficiency. Experimental results on English and Chinese datasets demonstrate that our parser increases unlabeled and labeled attachment scores by approximately 2% over traditional baselines. Notably, the system is capable of parsing more than 1,000 sentences per second while maintaining an unlabeled attachment score of 92.2% on the English Penn Treebank, providing a powerful balance of speed and precision.
humanized_abstract: Traditional transition-based dependency parsers require manual feature engineering and typically have to make use of millions of sparse indicator features. These models tend to have a high feature extraction cost and suffer from poor generalization performance. In this paper, we propose a new neural network-based model for greedy transition-based dependency parsers. We embed words, POS tags and dependencies into low dimensional dense vector spaces and then learn a compact set of dense features using a novel cube activation function to model high-order interactions. The parser significantly outperforms previous models in terms of accuracy and speed. We report +2% (4% absolute) unlabeled and labeled attachment scores on English and Chinese. Our parser can parse more than 1,000 sentences per second while maintaining an unlabeled attachment score of 92.2% on the popular English Penn Treebank dataset.

The parser significantly outperforms previous models in terms of accuracy and speed. We report +2% (4% absolute) unlabeled and labeled attachment scores on English and Chinese. Our parser can parse more than 1,000 sentences per second while maintaining an unlabeled attachment score of 92.2% on the popular English Penn Treebank dataset.
